content:
5 min read
Just now

--

URL state means storing UI state, such as filters, the active tab, a search query, or pagination, in the URL query string instead of React useState, so a refresh, a shared link, or the browser back button restores exactly what the user saw. In the Next.js App Router I read it with the useSearchParams hook or the async searchParams prop, write it with router.replace, and reach for the nuqs library when I want a type-safe, useState-shaped API. The trap that cost me an afternoon: a component calling useSearchParams must sit inside a React Suspense boundary or the production build fails.
A dashboard I built kept its filter panel in useState. Users would filter to a subset, refresh to pull fresh data, and lose every selection. The fix was not more state management, it was less: move the state a user expects to survive a reload into the URL, where the browser persists it for free.
When should I store state in the URL instead of useState. Store state in the URL when the user expects it to survive a refresh, a shared link, or a bookmark, which covers filters, the active tab, a search term, sort order, and pagination. Keep state in useState when it is ephemeral, like a hover state, whether a dropdown is open, or an unsaved form draft. My test is a single question: if the user copies this URL and sends it to a colleague, should the colleague see the same view? If yes, it belongs in the URL. URL state also removes a class of synchronization bugs, because there is one source of truth, the query string, instead of a useState value that can drift out of sync with the address bar.
How do I read search params in the Next.js App Router. There are two ways depending on where the code runs. In a client component, call the useSearchParams hook from next slash navigation, which returns a read-only URLSearchParams object, and read a key from it with a fallback default. In a server component you skip the hook entirely and read the searchParams prop that every page receives. In Next.js 15 that prop is a Promise, so you await it, which lets the server fetch the correct data before it renders anything. That server-side read is what enables real server-side filtering with no client fetch at all.
How do I write to the URL without triggering a full navigation. Build a fresh URLSearchParams from the current params, set your key on it, and call router.replace with the pathname and the new query string. Seeding the new params from the existing ones preserves every other query key on the page. The important detail is to use router.replace rather than router.push for filter and tab changes. push adds a history entry on every change, so the back button steps through each individual tweak instead of returning to the previous page, while replace keeps a single entry.
What is nuqs and what does useQueryState add. nuqs is a type-safe search-params state manager for React that turns the read plus router.replace dance into one useState-shaped hook. Its useQueryState hook takes a key and a parser and returns a value and setter tuple, except the value lives in the URL. The parsers are the real win: parseAsInteger, parseAsBoolean, parseAsArrayOf, parseAsStringEnum, and parseAsIsoDateTime convert the string in the URL into the type your component actually wants, and back again on write. Setting a value to null removes the key from the URL. In nuqs version 2 you wrap the app once in a NuqsAdapter from the next app adapter path, and you can batch several keys at once with useQueryStates.
Native searchParams or nuqs, which should I pick. Pick native useSearchParams for one or two string params, and nuqs when you have many typed params or want the boilerplate gone. Native gives you strings only, so you parse them yourself, needs no setup, and writes through a manual URLSearchParams and router.replace. nuqs gives you typed parsers for integers, booleans, arrays, enums, and dates, needs a one-time NuqsAdapter wrapper, exposes a useState-like tuple, batches many keys with useQueryStates, handles defaults with withDefault and clearOnDefault, and throttles rapid writes for you.
What breaks: Suspense, throttling, and stale server data. The failure that cost me time is the build aborting with the message that useSearchParams should be wrapped in a suspense boundary. During static rendering, any component that reads useSearchParams must sit inside a Suspense boundary, because the params are only known at request time; wrapping the reading component makes the build pass. Two more edges are worth knowing. Writing to the URL on every keystroke spams both the history and re-renders, so nuqs throttles URL updates by default and lets you raise the throttle in milliseconds, whereas with native code you debounce the write yourself. And whether a URL change re-runs your server components depends on shallow routing: nuqs defaults to shallow true, which updates the URL on the client without a server round-trip, so you set shallow false when the server needs the new value to refetch, for example when filtering happens in a server component through the searchParams prop.
A few common questions. On when to keep state in the URL versus useState: use the URL for state that should survive a refresh, a shared link, or a bookmark, such as filters, tabs, search, sort, and pagination, and use useState for ephemeral UI like open menus, hover, or an unsaved form draft. On whether you need nuqs: useSearchParams plus router.replace covers reading and writing, while nuqs adds typed parsers, a useState-like API, multi-key batching, and throttling, so native is enough for one or two string params and nuqs pays off for many typed params. On the build failure about a suspense boundary: during static rendering Next.js requires any component calling useSearchParams to be inside a Suspense boundary since the value is only known at request time, so wrap that component. On push versus replace for filter changes: use router.replace, because push adds a history entry per change so the back button walks through every tweak, while replace keeps one entry. And on whether updating the URL re-runs your server components: with native router.replace it does re-render the route server components, while with nuqs it depends on the shallow option, where the default shallow true is client only and shallow false triggers the server refetch.
Originally published at https://www.devya.dev/blogs/url-state-nextjs-app-router-field-notes — also on https://www.eng-ahmed.com/blog/url-state-nextjs-app-router-field-notes
